text.title: Blurred Roles Team Model
text.summary: Overlapping Venn diagram team structure where each role holds fluency in adjacent disciplines, replacing relay-race handoffs.

section.title: The Core Shift
section.p: Traditional product teams pass work in sequence: PM writes spec, designer designs, engineer builds, QA tests. AI-era teams operate as overlapping Venn diagrams. Each discipline participates earlier and across more phases. The overlap is intentional — it is the operating model.

rule.statement: Every Venn-overlap zone must have exactly one human DRI. Agents fill the zone but never own the decision.
rule.rationale: Without a DRI, parallel role-persona agents on the same problem produce contradictions and the team ships the loudest recommendation rather than the right one.

rule.statement: Require PMs to hold fluency in design, data, model behavior, distribution, and pricing — not just spec writing and roadmap ownership.
rule.rationale: As engineering throughput scales with AI, the bottleneck shifts to discovery, evaluation, and judgment. PMs without adjacent fluency become the constraint.

example.note: PM who can write SQL to validate a hypothesis, read a confusion matrix, and articulate a pricing model trade-off participates in all three overlap zones without creating handoffs.
PM who writes a spec and hands it to design, then waits for a handoff from engineering to review, is operating as a relay runner — creating latency at every baton pass.

rule.statement: Pair every "PM speaks design" expectation with a symmetric expectation on the other side. One-sided blurring breeds resentment.
rule.rationale: If PMs are expected to give design feedback but designers are not expected to track metrics, the blurring is extractive, not collaborative.

rule.statement: Keep one depth specialist per critical discipline (security, ML eval, accessibility). Full-blurring is an anti-pattern in deep domains.
rule.rationale: T-shaped fluency scales well; eliminating specialization entirely creates a team that is mediocre at everything and expert at nothing.

section.title: PM:Engineer Ratio
section.p: Andrew Ng observed at Sequoia AI Ascent 2024 that AI-augmented teams invert the traditional ratio: 2 PMs per 1 Engineer replaces the traditional 1 PM per 4–8 Engineers. This holds when engineering throughput is heavily agent-assisted and the real bottleneck is discovery, evals, and prioritization — not coding.

rule.statement: Treat the 2:1 PM:Engineer ratio as a hypothesis per team, not a universal target. Measure agent-assisted velocity separately from baseline engineering throughput.
rule.rationale: The inversion only holds when the engineer is heavily AI-augmented. Measuring on baseline throughput leads to over-hiring PMs and under-building.

rule.statement: Re-audit role overlap quarterly using commit/PR-author distribution as the truth signal, not self-reported role descriptions.
rule.rationale: Self-reported role splits in surveys are systematically wrong by ~30%. Git shortlog surfaces who actually touches which areas of the codebase.

antipattern.description: Blurred roles defined in a Notion page but not reflected in actual work assignment or code ownership.
antipattern.reason: Blurred roles drift back to silos under deadline pressure. The quarterly audit is the forcing function that prevents reversion.

text.reference: Emit per-author file-area distribution from git history to surface actual role boundaries
